name: Byblos Adonis
description: Byblos, an ancient Phoenician city, held immense religious significance, particularly in connection with the cult of Adonis. It was believed to be the site where Adonis, the god of beauty, desire, and vegetation, was tragically killed by a wild boar. The river Nahr Ibrahim (Adonis River) which flows from the mountains near Byblos, was central to the Adonis myth. Each year, the river would turn red, a phenomenon attributed to the blood of Adonis flowing into the river from the mountains where he was slain. This annual reddening was seen as a symbol of Adonis's death and resurrection, and a major festival was held in Byblos to mourn his death and celebrate his rebirth. The cult of Adonis in Byblos involved elaborate rituals and processions, reflecting the cyclical nature of life, death, and renewal. The lamentation for Adonis was a central part of the festival, with women wailing and tearing their hair in mourning. The sacred grove near Byblos was believed to be the burial place of Adonis, further solidifying the city's importance as a center of his worship. The myth of Adonis and the rituals performed in Byblos demonstrate the Phoenician understanding of the interconnectedness of nature, fertility, and the divine, and the importance of seasonal cycles in their religious beliefs. The story highlights the power of love and loss, and the enduring hope for renewal even in the face of death. Byblos, as the locus of these events, became a powerful symbol of these themes.
subtitle: Site of Adonis's death and rebirth

keyMyths.title: The Death of Adonis
keyMyths.description: Adonis, while hunting, was gored to death by a wild boar. His blood stained the river Nahr Ibrahim, turning it red.
keyMyths.source: Various ancient texts and later interpretations

keyMyths.title: Aphrodite's Lament
keyMyths.description: Aphrodite, heartbroken by Adonis's death, mourned him deeply. Her tears are said to have sprung forth anemone flowers where they fell.
keyMyths.source: Various ancient texts and later interpretations

sources.author: Walter Burkert
sources.description: Discusses the influence of Near Eastern cults, including Adonis, on Greek religion.
sources.text: Discusses the influence of Near Eastern cults, including Adonis, on Greek religion.
